issues.issue: Choice-4 (Alvarez schedule question) offers two options but both route to the identical next scene, and the substantive difference is only reflected in a short dialogue branch rather than any structural consequence.
issues.impact: Players may feel the choice is cosmetic since the scene graph doesn't diverge, slightly undercutting the sense of agency right before the climax.
issues.fix: Let option 'b' (move to smaller slot) actually alter scene-8 framing (e.g., Alex arrives already relocated, changing the tone of the direct question) so the branch has a visible downstream fingerprint, not just a line of dialogue.

issues.issue: Scene 1 (announcement) is fairly long and expository relative to later scenes, front-loading a lot of character introductions (Maya's committee comment, Jordan's playlist, Alex's reaction) before the first real choice.
issues.impact: Slight lag before player agency kicks in; low-engagement risk for players wanting to act quickly.
issues.fix: Trim 1-2 lines of setup banter or move the first choice earlier (e.g., let Maya's committee pitch happen in the same beat) to tighten time-to-first-choice.

issues.issue: Only 3 named outcome branches are defined despite 5 sequential binary/ternary choices (2^4 x 3 = 48 theoretical paths), meaning most choice combinations collapse into the same 'full-status' or 'full-authentic' outcome without unique acknowledgment.
issues.impact: Players who mix strategies (e.g., quiet at the leak but decisive backstage) won't see their specific pattern reflected, reducing the payoff of replaying with different choice combinations.
issues.fix: Add 1-2 more mixed-outcome variants (e.g., 'Public bravado, private doubt') or at least vary closing dialogue lines based on partial trait totals rather than only the three canonical paths.

issues.issue: No explicit signal to new players that earlier small choices (committee, curation) accumulate toward the big leak/backstage moments — the causal chain is implicit only.
issues.impact: First-time players may not realize early 'low stakes' choices are quietly building toward the emotionally loaded finale, understating their weight.
issues.fix: A subtle INTERNAL line after choice-2a/2b hinting 'this isn't the last time this will matter' would reinforce stakes without breaking immersion.

strengths: Excellent escalating structure: low-stakes committee choice → mid-stakes curation choice → high-stakes public leak choice → reflective adult-mediated choice → intimate capstone choice, mirroring rising tension well
Choice-3 (the leak) is a genuinely well-designed three-way dilemma (loud defense / quiet support / silence) with no clean 'correct' answer and clear trait signaling for different playstyles
Strong use of recurring motifs (four taps, half-late laugh) that pay off across branches, rewarding attentive players

The 'Late, But Real' outcome is a smart design choice — it explicitly validates recovery/redemption arcs rather than punishing an early misstep permanently, which is rare and valuable in branching narrative design
Backstage direct question (choice-5) creates a satisfying narrative rhyme/callback to the leak scene, giving the finale real weight without needing new mechanics
Good variety in scene length and emotional register — quiet library scenes contrast well with the loud cafeteria set-piece
recommendations: Add a small mid-episode agency beat (e.g., a brief exchange with Jordan) to break up the long stretch between choice-2 and choice-3 where the player is otherwise mostly reactive
Consider surfacing a lightweight trait/relationship indicator after major choices so players can track their 'build' without needing to infer it from dialogue tone alone
Expand the outcomes table to at least acknowledge 2 more hybrid paths so replay experimentation feels rewarded
Slightly tighten scene-1 dialogue to get to the first choice faster

summary.verdict: A well-crafted, emotionally coherent episode with a strong central dilemma (the cafeteria leak) and a satisfying redemption path, but replayability and mid-episode agency are only good, not great — no blocking issues.
keyIssues: Choice-4 lacks structural divergence despite dialogue differences
Outcome variety undercounts the actual choice-combination space
Slow time-to-first-choice in the opening scene
topStrengths: Escalating stakes structure culminating in a genuinely dilemma-rich leak scene
Strong callback structure (four taps, half-late laugh, direct-question rhyme) rewarding attentive play
niceToHave: Give choice-4 a visible structural consequence, not just dialogue flavor
Add hybrid outcome variants for underrepresented choice combinations
Trim opening scene to reach first choice faster
Add a lightweight progress/relationship signal after major choices
